perturbation.original_text: Purchaser will cause the Purchaser Licensees to use the Licensed Trademarks following the Initial Closing Date with appropriate legends as required under applicable Law. Purchaser agrees that the quality of all Seller Excluded Products and any other products and services marketed or sold by the Purchaser Licensees under the Licensed Trademarks will conform to at least the level of quality of the Enterprise Products as currently provided by the Seller Entities as of the Initial Closing Date. Purchaser will (and will cause each Purchaser Licensee to) reasonably cooperate with Seller in facilitating Seller's control of such quality, permit reasonable inspection of the Purchaser Licensees' operations (upon reasonable notice and during normal business hours) solely with respect to their respective use of the Licensed Trademarks (if any), and supply Seller with specimens of any of their respective uses of the Licensed Trademarks (if any), including such specimens that are advertising or marketing materials, upon Seller's reasonable request, at Seller's expense.
perturbation.changed_text: Purchaser will endeavor to cause the Purchaser Licensees to use the Licensed Trademarks following the Initial Closing Date with legends. Purchaser expects that the quality of all Seller Excluded Products and any other products and services marketed or sold by the Purchaser Licensees under the Licensed Trademarks will generally conform to the level of quality of the Enterprise Products as currently provided by the Seller Entities as of the Initial Closing Date. Purchaser will consider cooperating with Seller regarding Seller's control of such quality, and may allow inspection of the Purchaser Licensees' operations, if convenient, with reasonable notice and during normal business hours, and provide Seller with samples of uses of the Licensed Trademarks upon request, if feasible, at Seller's expense.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the quality control obligations for the Purchaser. Replacing 'will cause' with 'will endeavor to cause' and 'expects that the quality...will conform' introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of maintaining the quality of products sold under the licensed trademarks. Similarly, replacing 'reasonably cooperate' with 'will consider cooperating' and 'permit reasonable inspection' with 'may allow inspection, if convenient' further weakens the Purchaser's obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications weaken the obligation to maintain consistent quality and cooperate with quality control. This contradicts the Lanham Act, which requires trademark owners to maintain control over the quality of goods or services sold under the trademark to avoid misleading consumers. Failure to adequately control quality can lead to the mark being deemed abandoned, as the public may be deceived into thinking they are receiving a product of similar quality to the original. The modified language allows for inconsistent quality, which could deceive consumers and damage the seller's brand.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities regarding quality control of licensed trademarks, potentially contradicting trademark law. The original text imposed obligations on the Purchaser to 'cause' licensees to use trademarks with appropriate legends, maintain quality, cooperate with Seller's quality control, and permit inspections. The changed text weakens these obligations by using terms like 'endeavor,' 'expects,' 'generally conform,' 'consider cooperating,' and 'may allow inspection,' making the quality control and cooperation less enforceable. This weakens the trademark holder's ability to ensure consistent quality of goods sold under the trademark, potentially misleading consumers. While the provided snippets do not explicitly address quality control, the Law Explanation states that weakening quality control contradicts the Lanham Act, which requires trademark owners to maintain control over the quality of goods or services sold under the trademark. This aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, as the ambiguous obligation makes it difficult to enforce quality standards.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that from time to time, at the reasonable request and expense of the other party, it shall execute and deliver such other documents and take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request to effectuate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement (including any short form documentation evidencing the licenses granted by any Seller Party hereunder or other documentation to perfect or record the rights granted hereunder in the Transferred IP or Licensed IP in any jurisdiction throughout the world).
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that from time to time, if deemed necessary, at the request and expense of the other party, it may attempt to execute and deliver such other documents and consider taking such other actions as the other party may suggest to generally effectuate the transactions referenced in this IP Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation for both parties to execute documents and take actions necessary to effectuate the IP Agreement. The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens this obligation by replacing 'agrees that...it shall execute and deliver' with 'agrees that...it may attempt to execute and deliver' and 'take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request' with 'consider taking such other actions as the other party may suggest.' The addition of 'if deemed necessary' adds another layer of subjectivity, making the obligation less enforceable. The reference to 'generally effectuate the transactions referenced' is vague and does not specifically address the perfection or recording of rights, further reducing the enforceability of this section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 9 of the UCC governs secured transactions, including the creation and perfection of security interests in intellectual property. The original text ensured both parties would cooperate to perfect security interests (if needed) to protect the rights granted under the IP Agreement. The modified text, by making the actions discretionary and adding ambiguity, undermines the ability to create and perfect such security interests. UCC § 9-203 requires specific actions and documentation to create an enforceable security interest. The vague language in the modified text would likely be insufficient to meet these requirements, potentially leaving a party's rights unsecured and vulnerable to other creditors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 9-203. ATTACHMENT AND ENFORCEABILITY OF SECURITY INTEREST; PROCEEDS; SUPPORTING OBLIGATIONS; FORMAL REQUISITES. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 9-203. ATTACHMENT AND ENFORCEABILITY OF SECURITY INTEREST; PROCEEDS; SUPPORTING OBLIGATIONS; FORMAL REQUISITES. (a) [Attachment.] A security interest attaches to collateral when it becomes enforceable against the debtor with respect to the collateral, unless an agreement expressly postpones the time of attachment. (b) [Enforceability.] Except as otherwise provided in subsections (c) through (i), a security interest is enforceable against the debtor and third parties with respect to the collateral only if : (1) value has been given; (2) the debtor has rights in the collateral or the power to transfer rights in the collateral to a secured party ; and (3) one of the following conditions is met: (A) the debtor has authenticated a security agreement that provides a description of the collateral and, if the security interest covers timber to be cut, a description of the land concerned; (B) the collateral is not a certificated security and is in the possession of the secured party under Section 9-313 pursuant to the debtor 's security agreement; (C) the collateral is a certificated security in registered form and the security certificate has been delivered to the secured party under Section 8-301 pursuant to the debtor 's security agreement; or (D) the collateral is deposit accounts , electronic chattel paper , investment property , or letter-of-credit rights , and the secured party has control under Section 9-104 , 9-105 , 9-106 , or 9-107 pursuant to the debtor 's security agreement. (c) [Other UCC provisions.] Subsection (b) is subject to Section 4-210 on the security interest of a collecting bank , Section 5-118 on the security interest of a letter-of-credit issuer or nominated person, Section 9-110 on a security interest arising under Article 2

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of the agreement, creating a contradiction based on the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. The original text used definitive language ('shall execute and deliver') to ensure cooperation in effectuating the IP Agreement. The changed text weakens this obligation by using discretionary language ('may attempt to execute and deliver') and subjective criteria ('if deemed necessary'). The Law Explanation notes that UCC § 9-203 requires specific actions and documentation to create an enforceable security interest. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 9-203, specifies conditions for a security interest to be enforceable, including authentication of a security agreement. The modified text's vague language ('generally effectuate the transactions referenced') is unlikely to meet these requirements, making it difficult to create and perfect security interests. This ambiguity creates a legal contradiction because it introduces an ambiguous obligation, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall (and shall cause the other members of its Group and its Affiliates to) hold the Source Code, trade secrets and other confidential information licensed to any of them under this IP Agreement in confidence, and shall protect the confidentiality thereof using at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own similar confidentiality or proprietary information, but in no event using less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will aim to (and encourage other members of its Group and its Affiliates to) generally treat the Source Code, trade secrets, and other confidential information licensed to any of them under this IP Agreement as confidential, and will endeavor to protect the confidentiality thereof, potentially using care.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear and strong obligation to maintain confidentiality. The modified text weakens this obligation considerably by: (1) Replacing "shall (and shall cause...to)" with "will aim to (and encourage...to)" - turning a mandatory action into a goal or suggestion. (2) Adding "generally treat...as confidential" - introducing ambiguity as to the scope and consistency of the confidentiality obligation. (3) Replacing "shall protect the confidentiality thereof using at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own similar confidentiality or proprietary information, but in no event using less than a reasonable degree of care" with "will endeavor to protect the confidentiality thereof, potentially using care" - removing the standard of care (at least the same degree, but no less than reasonable) and making the use of any care optional. This drastically weakens the enforceable confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. To be protected under the DTSA, reasonable measures must be taken to keep the information secret. The original text provided a reasonable standard of care (at least the same degree they use for their own information, but no less than reasonable). The modified text removes this standard, making any effort to protect the information discretionary and potentially insufficient. This would jeopardize the ability to claim trade secret protection under the DTSA, as failing to take reasonable measures negates trade secret status. The term 'potentially using care' fails to meet this threshold requirement for actively protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intention of the DTSA, which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. While there are no scraped snippets to directly reference, the Law Explanation states the original contract provided a reasonable standard of care while the modified text removes this standard, making protection discretionary. This directly undermines the requirement for 'reasonable measures' because the modified text makes using any care optional, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under the DTSA. The original contract also stated 'shall' but the revised contract replaced this with 'will aim to', creating an ambiguous and unenforceable obligation.
